text: The patient is positioned supine, with laparoscopic ports placed according to a diagram. After port placement, the patient is tilted head down and right shoulder up. The omentum is moved into the right upper quadrant above the liver, and the small bowel is positioned into the left side of the abdomen.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the ascending colon, starting under the iliacolic pedicle. An initial incision into the peritoneum reveals a bloodless plane, which is dissected using hooked diathermy and blunt dissection. As the dissection progresses towards the duodenum, retroperitoneal structures and fascia are separated from the mesocolon.

The surgeon identifies and separates the duodenum from the mesocolon using scissors and blunt dissection, avoiding diathermy to prevent thermal injury. Important retroperitoneal structures such as the IVC, gonadal vessels, and ureter are visible. The duodenum is completely mobilized, and the medial to lateral dissection continues using a harmonic scalpel and gentle sweeping motion to separate the retroperitoneal fascia from the mesocolic envelope, with a clear color delineation marking the borders between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum.

A swab is placed above the mobilized duodenum to serve as a landmark. The video shows the mobilized duodenum, the head of the pancreas, and retroperitoneal structures that have been separated from the mesocolon.

The surgeon begins vessel control by skeletonizing the ileocolic pedicle and exposing the superior mesenteric vein. Counter traction is applied by pulling the pedicle to the patient's right side, creating tension on the peritoneal coverings for easy division. Haemlock clips are placed proximally and distally on the vessel, which is then divided between the clips. Additional clips are placed on nearby structures to minimize bleeding risk. The ileocolic artery and vein are controlled with these clips due to their close proximity.

The surgeon proceeds to divide the tributaries of the superior mesenteric vein following the division of the ileocolic pedicle. The branches are identified and clips are applied as proximal as possible to ensure maximum lymph node yield, adhering to the principle of central venous ligation.

The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection above the duodenum, creating a visible window into the lesser sac. The exposed duodenum and head of the pancreas are clearly visible. Final vessels, likely tributaries of the gastrocolonic trunk, are safely ligated.

The surgeon elevates the cecum and appendix to create tension, facilitating entry into an avascular plane for dissection. Using traction and counter traction, the fibers between the posterior fascia and the mesocolic envelope of the cecum are divided. A window is created, joining the subalial dissection to the earlier medial to lateral dissection, with care taken to avoid injuring the nearby gonadal vessels and ureter. The dissection continues until reaching the swab placed over the duodenum.
The assistant pulls the colon to the patient's left side, creating tension that facilitates lateral mobilisation. The surgeon continues dissection while preserving the intact mesocolic envelope.

The surgeon continues lateral mobilisation until the right lobe of the liver is visible, marking the end of this stage. The omentum is separated from the colon by creating a window into the lesser sac, which is extended medially to laterally. The fully mobilised colon allows for safe division of remaining vessels, likely including the right gastroepiploic vein.

The colon is positioned at the patient's midline, revealing underlying posterior structures and haemlocks marking the centrally ligated vessels. The surgical specimen is extracted through a small midline incision with an Alexis wound protector. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed using TLS-CMT and TA-CMT linear staplers, following the standard Barcelona technique.
